p.a:

Attribution 4.0 International License

presentation.title: APIs für die AI Nutzung: Wie geht das?
presentation.toc: AI stellt neue Anforderungen an die Auffindbarkeit und Nutzbarkeit von APIs. Wir schauen darauf, wie APIs bisher entworfen und verwaltet wurden, und welche neuen Aspekte für die Unterstützung von AI hinzukommen. Die Unterschiede zum bisherigen Vorgehen sind nicht grundsätzlicher Art, aber gross genug, dass sie Änderungen im Umgang mit APIs erfordern.

img.title: betterCode
img.style: float: right ; clear : both ; width : 30% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ;

ul.li: Kurator & Trainer iSAQB Modul API
Independent Expert for APIs & Integration Architectures
Lead Developer / Technical Architect / Trainer
Author & Speaker
Advisory Board Member for API Conference & betterCode() API

img.title: OpenAPI Initiative
img.style: float: right ; clear : both ; width : 30% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ;

ul.li: Kurator & Trainer iSAQB Modul API
Head of Enterprise Strategy at Jentic
Ambassador for the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)

part.title: API Design & Description in the Age of AI

ul.li: Requirements Analysis
Talk to your consumers
Outside-in approach
API First
Imagine you are on a greenfield
Do not create APIs based on existing systems or use cases
API Design
Use iterative approach of design – review - design
Implement good practices for API Design
Make sure to have API Design Guidelines in your organization
Focus on usability, consistency, comprehensibility, extensibility
Use naming conventions and consistent endpoint structures
Favor generic APIs over support for specific consumers / use cases
API specification
Use standard description languages, like OpenAPI or AsyncAPI
Make sure to document as many aspects as possible, including all response status codes, parameters and attributes
API documentation
Technical details missing in the API specification
Information explaining how endpoints work
Semantics of data structures and runtime behaviour
SDKs, quick-start guides
Make APIs discoverable
Register APIs in dev portals, API marketplaces, or internal catalogs
Provide metadata, tags and standardized authentication details
Offer sandbox environments and test credentials
Maintain backwards compatibility
AI Coding Agents generate code that consumes APIs
AI Business Agents discover and consume APIs autonomously
What does all this mean for API providers?
What changes, what stays the same?

part.title: Exposing API Landscapes for AI Consumption

slide.title: AI Integration Is the New Moat

ul.li: APIs were typically consumed by developers
Developers write code that often fills DX gaps
LLMs have more limited context than developers
Coding agents shouldn't depend on developers selecting APIs
Business agents need to fully understand APIs

ol.li: Improve descriptions → easy, no code change, immediately more agent-friendly
Improve definitions (backwards-compatible) → change the API, don't break consumers
Revise the API → new API; consumers must migrate
Add workflows on top → orchestration above existing APIs

ul.li: Most API descriptions need quality improvements anyway
Better descriptions benefit everyone, not just AI agents
Complete and meaningful operation summaries
Comprehensive examples for all operations
Clear error handling
Consistent terminology and naming
Good DX is the foundation for good AX

slide.title: Level 1b: Now There's AX Homework, Too

ul.li: AI agents need information developers don't typically require:
Everything that developers usually just know
Treat every API like an external one for developers with no context
OpenAPI Initiative (OAI) might add AX-specific fields
Until then, leverage description fields strategically
Improve the API itself without breaking existing consumers
Add optional fields and parameters
Enrich responses with new optional structures
Add new endpoints alongside existing ones
Tighten constraints; never loosen contracts
Works well in well-governed API landscapes
Morphs into Level 3 in brittle or ungoverned API landscapes
Declarative: define the data flow and success criteria
OpenAPI Initiative (OAI) standard for API workflows
References operationIds from OpenAPI/AsyncAPI documents

slide.title: You Can't Improve What You Don't Measure

ul.li: Spectral & Co. aren't all you need
API Scorecard scores your APIs across six dimensions
Repo: github.com/jentic/jentic-api-scorecard
The pitch: APIs are old, MCP is the future
Wrap anything in MCP → call it AI-ready
Skip the hard API work → straight to AI
Just start fresh after 10 years of API landscape work?
Auto-generate MCP from OpenAPI for every API
Context window explosion on real workloads
Bad descriptions → bad MCP
AI still has to invent workflows across endpoints
Now your agents get to have a poor integration experience

slide.title: Problem 2: Investing in the MCP Layer

ul.li: Hand-craft MCP servers, design the best tools
The investment goes into the MCP layer
The capability layer underneath remains untouched
Better capabilities benefit everyone, not just MCP
Everybody's integration experience should benefit
Skip the API work → jump to MCP → call it AI-readiness
This is the wrong place to invest from a strategy perspective
MCP should be a thin layer, not a fat one
Invest in your APIs and then expose them through MCP

slide.title: Aligned with the iSAQB API Module

ul.li: Covered in the CPSA Advanced Level: API Module
API Design, Description, Lifecycle
API Security & APIs at Scale
Background reading (iSAQB blog posts)

q.a: APIs as Business Assets: Designing for Scale, Governance, and AI

img.title: This is the End
img.style: float: right ; height : 65% ; margin : 0% 6% 4% 6% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;
